message:
fix(widgets/inspector): clip widget-bound overlay to frame buffer bounds

The inspector overlay renders widget bounds and labels directly
into the active frame buffer, but the previous implementation
trusted the widget's reported `area` to be fully contained within
the drawing buffer. That assumption fails whenever a widget is
partially outside the viewport — scrolled lists, virtualized
content, overlapping panes — and the overlay would then either
read/write out-of-bounds cells (silently dropped by
`Buffer::get_mut`, so no crash but also no useful output) or
produce visually wrong highlights that reached past the current
terminal size.

## Clipping plumbing

`render_widget_bounds` now computes
`let clip = area.intersection(&frame.buffer.bounds());` once at
entry and short-circuits when the clip is empty. It then threads
`clip` through `render_widget_bound`, `draw_rect_outline`, and
`draw_label` so each primitive can reject out-of-bounds pixels.

`render_widget_bound` also guards against empty widget areas
before drawing the outline *and* label (previously only the
outline was guarded, so a zero-width widget could still push an
empty `[name]` label at its corner), and continues to recurse into
children regardless so that a clipped parent does not hide a
visible child.

## `draw_rect_outline` edge-by-edge clipping

Each of the four edges is now drawn only when the edge's y (top /
bottom) or x (left / right) lies within the clip rectangle, and the
horizontal edges use `row_cells_mut_span(y, clipped.x,
clipped.right())` to get a single mutable slice for the intersected
span instead of walking cell-by-cell with `get_mut` on out-of-bound
coordinates. The vertical edges iterate `y0..y1 = clipped.y..
clipped.bottom()` instead of the unclipped `y..=bottom`. The
`import` list switched from `use ftui_render::cell::{Cell,
PackedRgba}` to just `PackedRgba` because the `Cell` import was no
longer needed after the span API change.

## `draw_label` clipping + bracket layout

`draw_label` now clips on two levels: first a fast `clip.contains(x,
y)` test for the label anchor, then a computed `label_width =
label_len.min(area.width).min(clip.right().saturating_sub(x))` so
that a label straddling the clip boundary is rendered up to the
clip edge and no further. The label text is also drawn in three
`draw_text_span` calls — `"["`, `name`, `"]"` — so the closing
bracket can be placed at exactly the correct column even when the
name is truncated by the clip, instead of the old single
`format!("[{name}]")` path that would lose the closing bracket
entirely on a clipped label.
